distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
questions.question: The sum of the interior angles of an \(n\)-sided convex polygon is given by which formula?
questions.hint: Consider how many non-overlapping triangles are formed by drawing diagonals from one vertex.
questions.explanation: The sum of interior angles is \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) because an \(n\)-gon can be divided into \(n-2\) triangles, each summing to \(180^\circ\). Option A gives \(n \times 180^\circ\) and would be the sum of interior and exterior angles together.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of measure of interior angles of n sided polygon is equal to (n-2) × 180°

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of interior angles formula \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) calculates the total internal angle measure of any convex \(n\)-gon.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: By drawing non-overlapping diagonals from one vertex, any \(n\)-gon is divided into \(n-2\) triangles. Since each triangle has an angle sum of \(180^\circ\), the polygon's total is \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of cutting a shape into triangles from one corner. If a shape has \(n\) sides, you get \(n-2\) triangles, each worth \(180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A convex polygon with \(n\) sides can be triangulated into \(n-2\) distinct triangles. Summing each triangle's \(180^\circ\) yields \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) for the polygon's interior angles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inductively, adding a triangle to an \(n\)-gon increases side count by 1 and angle sum by \(180^\circ\). Base case: triangle (\(n=3\), sum \(180^\circ\)) gives general formula \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that a triangle has 3 sides and interior sum \(180^\circ\).
Note that an \(n\)-gon can be split into \(n-2\) triangles.
Multiply triangle sum by \(n-2\) to obtain polygon sum.
misconceptions: Using \(n \times 180^\circ\) is incorrect because that counts each interior angle twice when adding exterior angles as well.
Some think the formula is \((n-1) \times 180^\circ\), but that would undercount by one triangle.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sum of interior angles: \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\). Works for all convex polygons.

glossary_entry.definition: The total measure of all interior angles of a polygon, equal to \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) for an \(n\)-gon.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for the sum of interior angles of an \(n\)-gon?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It gives the sum of interior angles of an \(n\)-sided convex polygon. Example: hexagon (\(n=6\)): \((6-2) \times 180^\circ = 720^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The total measure of all interior angles of a polygon, equal to \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) for an \(n\)-gon.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often misapply \(n \times 180^\circ\). Emphasize triangulation from one vertex.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: identify regular polygons and solve problems on area and perimeter
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The formula is \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) because \(n-2\) triangles fit in an \(n\)-gon.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.question: What property defines a convex polygon?

options.A: Every interior angle measures less than \(180^\circ\).
options.B: At least one interior angle measures more than \(180^\circ\).
options.C: All sides are equal in length.

questions.hint: Think about whether any vertex points inward toward the interior.
questions.explanation: A convex polygon has all interior angles less than \(180^\circ\), so all vertices point outward. Option B describes a concave polygon. Options C and D describe regular polygons, not convexity in general.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.content_anchor: convex polygon when the measure of each interior angle is less than 180 degrees

explanations_tiered.definition: A convex polygon is a polygon where every interior angle is strictly less than \(180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convex polygons bulge outward; no indentations exist. Any line segment connecting two points inside a convex polygon stays entirely inside the polygon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A convex shape has no dents. If you stretch a rubber band around it, the band touches every corner.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a convex polygon, all vertices point outward, creating interior angles each smaller than \(180^\circ\). No vertex points inward toward the polygon's interior.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally, a polygon is convex if for any two points inside the polygon, the entire segment connecting them lies inside. Equivalently, all interior angles are < \(180^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Check each interior angle of the polygon.
If every angle is less than \(180^\circ\), the polygon is convex.
If any angle exceeds \(180^\circ\), the polygon is concave.
misconceptions: Some believe that equal sides determine convexity, but a rhombus is convex while a dart (concave quadrilateral) has equal sides but a reflex angle.
Convex does not mean regular; rectangles are convex but not equilateral unless square.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Convex polygon: all interior angles < \(180^\circ\). No inward dents.

glossary_entry.definition: A polygon where all interior angles measure less than \(180^\circ\) and all vertices point outward.

flashcard.front: What condition makes a polygon convex?
flashcard.back: Every interior angle must be less than \(180^\circ\). No reflex angles allowed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can you tell a convex polygon from a concave one?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Convex: all interior angles < \(180^\circ\). Concave: at least one interior angle > \(180^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A polygon where each interior angle is less than \(180^\circ\) and no vertex points inward.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use real-world examples: a stop sign (convex) vs. a dart shape (concave).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. A convex polygon has every interior angle less than \(180^\circ\); option B describes concave polygons.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
questions.question: What is the sum of the exterior angles of any convex polygon, one exterior angle taken at each vertex?
questions.hint: Consider walking around the polygon; the total turn at all vertices is one full rotation.
questions.explanation: The sum of exterior angles (one per vertex) of any convex polygon is always \(360^\circ\), independent of the number of sides. Option B is the sum of interior angles; option D is the sum of linear pairs at each vertex.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of the measure of exterior angles of a polygon is 360°

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon, taking one exterior angle at each vertex, is \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: As you walk around a convex polygon, the total change in direction (the sum of exterior angles) equals one full revolution, or \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine walking around a shape; you turn at each corner. After going around once, you've turned a full circle, \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each exterior angle supplements its adjacent interior angle to \(180^\circ\). Summing all exterior angles: \(n \times 180^\circ - (n-2) \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Algebraically, sum(ext) = \(n \times 180^\circ - (n-2) \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ\). Geometrically, the exterior angles represent the total turning of a traversal path.
reasoning_steps: Write the sum of interior-exterior linear pairs: \(n \times 180^\circ\).
Subtract the sum of interior angles \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\).
Result is \(360^\circ\).
misconceptions: Some think the sum depends on \(n\); it does not — it is always \(360^\circ\).
Confusing with interior angle sum: \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) is interior, not exterior.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sum of exterior angles (one per vertex) = \(360^\circ\) for any convex polygon.

glossary_entry.definition: The total measure of one exterior angle at each vertex of any convex polygon, always equal to \(360^\circ\).

flashcard.front: What is the sum of the exterior angles of a convex polygon?
flashcard.back: Always \(360^\circ\). For a triangle, \(120^\circ+120^\circ+120^\circ=360^\circ\); for a square, \(90^\circ\times4=360^\circ\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does the sum of exterior angles of a convex polygon depend on the number of sides?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No, it is always \(360^\circ\), regardless of \(n\). For a pentagon, each exterior angle is \(72^\circ\), sum = \(360^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The constant \(360^\circ\) total of exterior angles, one per vertex, for any convex polygon.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a physical polygon walk: the total turn is one circle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon is \(360^\circ\), independent of side count.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
questions.question: What two conditions must a polygon satisfy to be classified as a regular polygon?
questions.hint: Think about both angle measures and side lengths.
questions.explanation: A regular polygon must be both equiangular (all interior angles equal) and equilateral (all sides equal). Option B lacks equilateral condition; option C lacks equiangular condition.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: regular if it is equiangular (having equal angles) and equilateral (having equal sides)

explanations_tiered.definition: A regular polygon is a polygon that is both equiangular (all angles equal) and equilateral (all sides equal).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Regular polygons are highly symmetric; examples include equilateral triangles and squares. All regular polygons are convex.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A regular shape has all sides the same length and all corners the same angle — like a stop sign or a square.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Equiangular means every interior angle has identical measure; equilateral means every side length is identical. Both properties together define regularity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For an \(n\)-gon, equiangular forces each interior angle to be \(\frac{(n-2)\times180^\circ}{n}\); equilateral fixes side length \(s\). Together they enable formulas for area, apothem, and perimeter.
reasoning_steps: Check whether all interior angles are equal.
Check whether all side lengths are equal.
If both are true, the polygon is regular.
misconceptions: Some think all equilateral polygons are regular, but a rhombus is equilateral but not equiangular (unless square).
All equiangular polygons are regular? No: a rectangle is equiangular but not equilateral (unless square).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Regular polygon = equilateral + equiangular. Must have both.

glossary_entry.definition: A convex polygon with all sides equal and all interior angles equal.

flashcard.back: It must have all sides equal (equilateral) and all angles equal (equiangular). Example: a square.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What two properties define a regular polygon?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Equiangular (all angles equal) and equilateral (all sides equal).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A convex polygon that is both equiangular and equilateral.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Regular: equal sides AND equal angles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with rhombus (equilateral only) and rectangle (equiangular only).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Both conditions (equiangular and equilateral) are necessary; one without the other does not guarantee a regular polygon.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
questions.question: A regular octagon has each exterior angle measuring \(45^\circ\). What is the measure of each interior angle?
questions.hint: Interior and exterior angles at the same vertex are supplementary.
questions.explanation: Each interior angle is supplementary to its adjacent exterior angle: \(180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ\). Option A confuses interior with exterior; option B is a right angle, not applicable here.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: at each vertex of a polygon the sum of interior and exterior angles is 180°

explanations_tiered.definition: For any polygon, each interior angle and its adjacent exterior angle are supplementary (sum to \(180^\circ\)).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since the exterior angle of a regular polygon is given as \(45^\circ\), the interior angle is \(180^\circ-45^\circ=135^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: An interior and exterior angle at the same corner make a straight line, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Subtract the exterior from \(180^\circ\) to find the interior.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For a regular polygon, the interior and exterior angles are supplementary. Given exterior \(45^\circ\), interior = \(180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This relationship holds for any convex polygon: interior + exterior = \(180^\circ\) because they form a linear pair along the extended side.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall that interior and exterior angles at the same vertex are supplementary.
reasoning_steps: Identify the given exterior angle measure.
Apply the supplementary relationship: interior + exterior = \(180^\circ\).
Solve for interior angle by subtraction.

formula_analysis.name: Linear pair of interior and exterior angles

misconceptions: Some mistakenly think interior = exterior for regular polygons (only true for square or \(n=4\)).
Confusing exterior with interior: \(45^\circ\) is the exterior, not the interior.

glossary_entry.definition: Two angles whose measures sum to \(180^\circ\).

flashcard.front: If a regular octagon has exterior angle \(45^\circ\), what is its interior angle?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find the interior angle of a regular polygon from its exterior angle?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Interior = \(180^\circ - \text{exterior}\). For a regular decagon (exterior \(36^\circ\)), interior = \(144^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two angles that add to \(180^\circ\); interior and exterior angles at a vertex are supplementary.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce the linear pair concept with diagrams.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Interior = \(180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ\). Option A is the exterior itself, option B is incorrect.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
questions.hint: Each vertex can be paired with the midpoint of the opposite side in a symmetrical way.
questions.explanation: A regular pentagon has exactly 5 lines of symmetry, each passing through a vertex and the midpoint of the opposite side. Option A is for equilateral triangles; option C for regular hexagons.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
source_trace.content_anchor: regular pentagon has 5 lines of symmetry

explanations_tiered.definition: A line of symmetry divides a figure into two mirror-image halves. A regular pentagon has five such lines.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a regular \(n\)-gon, the number of lines of symmetry equals \(n\). A regular pentagon (\(n=5\)) thus has 5 lines of symmetry.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a regular pentagon and fold it. You can fold it in 5 different ways so both sides match perfectly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each line of symmetry in a regular pentagon goes from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. With 5 vertices, there are 5 distinct symmetry lines.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The symmetry group of a regular \(n\)-gon (dihedral group \(D_n\)) has \(n\) reflection symmetries. For odd \(n\), each axis passes through a vertex and the opposite side midpoint.
reasoning_steps: Recall: regular \(n\)-gon has \(n\) lines of symmetry.
Substitute \(n=5\) for pentagon.
Therefore, 5 lines of symmetry.
misconceptions: Some think a pentagon has only 3 lines (triangle analogy) or 6 lines (hexagon analogy).
Confusing with order of rotational symmetry, which is also 5 but distinct from reflection symmetry.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Regular \(n\)-gon: \(n\) lines of symmetry. Pentagon: 5 lines.

glossary_entry.definition: A line through a figure such that the figure is reflected onto itself.

flashcard.front: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
flashcard.back: 5. Each line goes from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many lines of symmetry does a regular \(n\)-gon have?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(n\) lines. A regular hexagon has 6, a regular octagon has 8.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A line that divides a figure into two congruent mirror-image halves.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students draw and fold to verify.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A regular pentagon has 5 lines of symmetry, one from each vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
questions.question: A convex polygon has an interior angle sum of \(1080^\circ\). How many sides does this polygon have?
questions.explanation: Using \((n-2) \times 180^\circ = 1080^\circ\), divide by \(180^\circ\) to get \(n-2 = 6\), so \(n = 8\). Option A gives \(720^\circ\); option D gives \(1260^\circ\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of interior angle of a polygon is 1,080°, how many sides? n=8

explanations_tiered.definition: The formula \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) gives the sum of interior angles of an \(n\)-gon.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Substitute the given sum into the formula and solve for \(n\) to find the number of sides.
explanations_tiered.beginner: We know \((n-2) \times 180 = 1080\). Divide 1080 by 180 to get 6, then add 2 to find \(n = 8\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Set \((n-2) \times 180^\circ = 1080^\circ\). Divide: \(n-2 = 1080/180 = 6\). Thus \(n = 8\). The polygon is an octagon.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Equation: \(180n - 360 = 1080 → 180n = 1440 → n = 8\). This works for any convex polygon.
reasoning_steps: Recall sum of interior angles formula.
Plug in given sum.
Solve for \(n\) using division and addition.
misconceptions: Some forget to add 2 after division, getting \(n=6\) instead of \(n=8\).
Others incorrectly set \(n \times 180^\circ = 1080^\circ\), finding \(n=6\) (triangle sum confusion).

flashcard.front: If a convex polygon's interior angles sum to \(1080^\circ\), how many sides does it have?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An eight-sided convex polygon; interior angle sum = \(1080^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize reverse application of the formula.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Solve \((n-2)\times180=1080 → n-2=6 → n=8\). Option A (6) gives sum 720°, option D (9) gives 1260°.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
questions.question: If a convex polygon has 12 sides, what is the sum of its exterior angles (one at each vertex)?
questions.hint: Recall that the sum of exterior angles is constant regardless of the number of sides.
questions.explanation: The sum of exterior angles (one per vertex) for any convex polygon is always \(360^\circ\), independent of side count. Options A, C, and D incorrectly depend on \(n\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of the measure of exterior angles of a polygon is 360°

explanations_tiered.definition: For any convex polygon, taking one exterior angle at each vertex, the total sum is always \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This result holds for all convex polygons, from triangles to dodecagons, independent of the number of sides.
explanations_tiered.beginner: No matter how many sides a shape has, the exterior angles always add up to one full turn: \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The sum of exterior angles does not depend on \(n\). Even for a 12-gon, the total is still \(360^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that exterior angle sum is constant for convex polygons.
Memorize or derive: \(360^\circ\) always.
Apply to 12-sided polygon: still \(360^\circ\).
misconceptions: Some incorrectly multiply \(n \times 180^\circ\) for exterior sum.
Others think each exterior angle is \(360/n\) and sum that incorrectly; but sum of those equals \(360\) exactly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Exterior angle sum = \(360^\circ\) for any convex polygon.

flashcard.front: A convex dodecagon (12 sides) — what is the sum of its exterior angles?
flashcard.back: \(360^\circ\). The sum does not depend on the number of sides.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does the sum of exterior angles of a convex polygon change if you increase the number of sides?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No. It is always \(360^\circ\), whether triangle, hexagon, or dodecagon.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A polygon with twelve sides and twelve vertices.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students to test with a triangle, square, pentagon to verify always 360°.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon is always \(360^\circ\), regardless of the number of sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
questions.question: What is the measure of each interior angle of a regular nonagon (9 sides)?
questions.explanation: Each interior angle = \(\frac{(9-2) \times 180^\circ}{9} = \frac{7 \times 180^\circ}{9} = 140^\circ\). Option D corresponds to a decagon; option B to an octagon.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: measure of each interior angle of n-sided regular polygon is (n-2)×180°/n

explanations_tiered.definition: Each interior angle of a regular \(n\)-gon is \(\frac{(n-2) \times 180^\circ}{n}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: For a 9-sided regular shape, first find total interior sum: \(7 \times 180 = 1260^\circ\). Divide by 9 corners gives \(140^\circ\) each.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This formula derives from dividing the total sum \((n-2)180\) equally among \(n\) congruent interior angles.
worked_solution: Step 2: Divide by number of sides: \(1260^\circ / 9 = 140^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Identify number of sides \(n=9\).
Compute total interior angle sum: \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\).
Divide total by \(n\) to get each interior angle.

formula_analysis.name: Each interior angle of regular polygon
formula_analysis.derivation: From sum of interior angles formula divided equally among n angles.

misconceptions: Some forget to divide, giving total sum \(1260^\circ\) as an answer.
Others incorrectly use \((n-2) \times 180^\circ / (n-2)\) or similar.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Each interior angle of regular nonagon = \(140^\circ\).

flashcard.front: Find each interior angle of a regular nonagon.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is each interior angle of a regular nonagon (9 sides)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A nine-sided polygon; regular nonagon interior angle = \(140^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(\frac{7 \times 180}{9} = 140^\circ\). Option D is for decagon.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.question: A polygon has one interior angle measuring \(210^\circ\). How should this polygon be classified?
questions.hint: Recall that convex polygons have all interior angles less than \(180^\circ\).
questions.explanation: A concave polygon has at least one interior angle greater than \(180^\circ\). Since \(210^\circ > 180^\circ\), the polygon is concave. Options A and B require all angles < \(180^\circ\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.content_anchor: concave polygon when there is at least one interior angle whose measure is more than 180 degrees

explanations_tiered.definition: A concave polygon has at least one interior angle greater than \(180^\circ\) (reflex angle).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since \(210^\circ\) exceeds \(180^\circ\), the polygon is concave. Convex polygons cannot contain any angle > \(180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a shape has a corner pointing inward (a dent), the angle inside at that dent is more than \(180^\circ\). That is a concave shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Convex polygons require all interior angles < \(180^\circ\). An angle of \(210^\circ\) is reflex, making the polygon concave.
explanations_tiered.advanced: A single reflex angle (\(>180^\circ\)) forces the polygon to be non-convex. The line segment between two points in such a polygon may exit the interior.
reasoning_steps: Check measure of the given interior angle.
Compare with \(180^\circ\).
If greater than \(180^\circ\), classify as concave.
misconceptions: Some think any large angle makes it convex; actually, larger than 180 means concave.
Confusing with exterior angle: a reflex interior angle corresponds to an exterior angle < 180.

glossary_entry.definition: A polygon with at least one interior angle greater than \(180^\circ\).

flashcard.front: A polygon has an interior angle of \(210^\circ\). Is it convex or concave?
flashcard.back: Concave, because any interior angle > \(180^\circ\) makes the polygon concave.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of polygon contains an interior angle greater than \(180^\circ\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Concave polygon. Convex polygons have all interior angles < \(180^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A polygon with at least one interior angle exceeding \(180^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show examples of star-shaped or dart-shaped concave polygons.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Any interior angle > \(180^\circ\) indicates a concave polygon; convex polygons have all angles < \(180^\circ\).
